item.title: Energy independence could make America more aggressive
item.description: Being a net exporter means that energy shocks no longer constrain US foreign policy in the ways they once did

item.title: Mandelson vetting scandal shows Starmer isn’t up to the job
item.description: Something has gone badly wrong in communication between Downing Street and government departments

item.title: What kind of rally is this?
item.description: Plus Treasuries’ declining convenience yield

item.title: How Trump turned Iran into a stationary bandit
item.description: Tehran’s new ability to charge tolls in the Gulf is a powerful bargaining chip

item.title: Why we must shield our children from social media
item.description: Protecting kids from techno-oligarchs is a legal and moral duty — and other governments should follow Spain’s example

item.title: Why Coutts is making an unexpected play for video games
item.description: The over 300-year-old lender is touting its status as a major banker to the sector

item.title: Welcome to the age of hoarding
item.description: As global economic uncertainty mounts, stockpiling is back

item.title: What will a new horticultural reign mean for London’s Royal Parks?
item.description: Matthew Pottage is using imaginative plantings, ‘conscious compromise’ and a sense of theatre to transform the green spaces

item.title: The divine right of Trump
item.description: President’s embrace of religious imagery and rhetoric is provoking a backlash

item.title: Mythos cyber scare signals the economics of AI scarcity
item.description: As capabilities of frontier models advance, gaining access to technology could become critically important

item.title: Monte dei Paschi drama has no heroes, only winners
item.description: Luigi Lovaglio’s survival at the world’s oldest bank is down to typical Italian wheeling and dealing

item.title: AI has an Awful Image problem
item.description: Modern-day Luddites are gaining ground because tech titans haven’t shown people how innovation will improve their lives

item.title: Sotheby’s demonstrates subtle art of wriggling out of a cash squeeze
item.description: Creative financial gymnastics and an art market recovery help paint a much brighter picture future

item.title: The costly voting structure for US funds needs an overhaul
item.description: The current outdated system was designed for a very different era

item.title: The great independence charade
item.description: Scottish and Welsh election results may reignite the debate but it is all one big pantomime

item.title: What’s really shocking about the second China shock?
item.description: While the Chinese export surge continues unabated, import volumes have been anaemic

item.title: It’s clear we won’t regulate AI for safety’s sake
item.description: As usual, governments will barely affect the trends that most affect our lives

item.title: The Iran war will damage the petrodollar
item.description: Grand bargain that has underpinned US Treasury demand since 1974 is coming undone

item.title: Does AI want to be free?
item.description: Plus, global imbalances and global crises

item.title: Hermès bumps up against luxury’s scarcity paradox
item.description: There is a tricky balance to strike between exclusivity and scale

item.title: Deterrence costs and we will all have to pay
item.description: There is a need to stop wishful thinking on defence expenditure

item.title: Pensions drawdown: can the 4 Per Cent Rule survive stagflation?
item.description: Ensuring a fund lasts at least 30 years is trickier with asset values falling and prices soaring

item.title: The NHS should keep Palantir for patients’ sake
item.description: The US technology company is opposed by some employees but its software is getting results

item.title: China to drive a weight-loss drug price war
item.description: The active ingredient semaglutide is starting to lose patent protection and China can produce GLP-1 related treatments at scale

item.title: ‘Megadeals’ defy market fears and volatile geopolitics
item.description: Boards should heed the lessons of the last record surge in big M&A in 2015
